name: Byelorussian SSR (1951-1991)

colors.symbolism: the blood of patriots and the communist banner
the forests and fields of Belarus
background of the traditional Belarusian ornament
the hammer, sickle and star — emblems of the workers and peasants under the leadership of the Communist Party

description: A horizontal red field with a green stripe across the lower third (2:1 ratio). At the hoist, a vertical band carries a traditional Belarusian red-on-white ornamental pattern. In the upper hoist, a gold hammer and sickle beneath a gold-bordered red five-pointed star.
history: Adopted on 25 December 1951 to replace the earlier all-red Byelorussian SSR flag with one bearing a more distinctive Belarusian identity. The decorative band at the hoist is based on a 1917 embroidered rushnyk (ceremonial towel) by Matrena Markevich. The flag remained in use until 19 September 1991, when Belarus declared independence and adopted the white-red-white flag of the Belarusian People's Republic. After a 1995 referendum, a modified version of this Soviet-era flag (without the hammer-and-sickle and star, and with the ornament colours reversed to red-on-white) was readopted as the flag of the Republic of Belarus.

features.symbolism: red for the heroic past and the struggle for socialism; green for the forests and rich nature of Belarus
the alliance of industrial workers and peasants under socialism
the leading role of the Communist Party and the five continents of the international workers' movement
a vertical white-and-red traditional Belarusian decorative pattern at the hoist, distinguishing the Byelorussian SSR flag from other Soviet republic flags
